findings.description: Precedence is inverted vs. the documented contract: lines 67-68 apply env first then file, so dict.update makes FILE win over ENV, but the module docstring (line 3) and load() docstring (line 65) both promise 'environment variables > config file'; any key set in both the file and an env var silently takes the file value. Fix: swap the two update() calls (file first, env second).

yaml.load(f) is called without a Loader; on PyYAML 6.x the Loader argument is mandatory, so this raises TypeError that is NOT caught, crashing _read_file() instead of returning {} and defeating the graceful-degradation intent evident in the surrounding missing-file/non-dict guards. (Also a security issue — unsafe deserialization — deferred to critic-security; fix: use yaml.safe_load.)

open() and yaml parsing are unguarded: a malformed YAML body (yaml.YAMLError) or any OSError/PermissionError/IsADirectoryError on open propagates out of load(), whereas the missing-file and non-dict-root cases are handled by returning {}; the intended 'bad config -> empty dict' degradation is therefore inconsistent and breaks on the most common real failure (syntactically invalid file). Fix: wrap read+parse in try/except returning {} with a logged error.

require() is broken by the DEFAULTS merge: it calls get() (which merges DEFAULTS) so any key with a default (db_url, log_level, worker_count, request_timeout_s, feature_flags) can NEVER raise even when neither file nor env supplied it; conversely a key explicitly set to YAML null yields None and wrongly raises 'missing'. 'Required' should mean explicitly provided, and a None/null value cannot be distinguished from absent. Fix: check membership against file+env (pre-default) and/or use a sentinel instead of None.

Flat env overlay can clobber a structured key with a coerced scalar: an env var such as APP_FEATURE_FLAGS=1 maps to name 'feature_flags' and overwrites the dict with int 1 (or a bool/str), after which feature_enabled() calls .get on a non-mapping and raises AttributeError; there is no type/shape reconciliation between sources. Fix: restrict env overlay to known scalar keys or validate type before overwrite.

feature_enabled() assumes feature_flags is a mapping (flags.get(...)), but file or env can set it to a YAML list/scalar; flags.get then raises AttributeError. The {} fallback only covers the totally-absent case, not a present-but-wrong-type value. Fix: guard with isinstance(flags, dict) before calling .get.

_coerce applies type inference blindly to every env value regardless of the target key's expected type: APP_DB_URL=true becomes boolean True and APP_DB_URL=5432 becomes int 5432 for a value that must stay a string, producing type drift between defaults (typed), file (typed), and env (string-coerced) sources for the same key. Numeric forms like '08' (ValueError -> stays string) vs '8' (-> int 8) also yield inconsistent types. Fix: coerce per declared key schema rather than guessing.

The 'config loaded from %s (%d keys)' INFO log fires on every load() call, and because get()/require()/feature_enabled() each call load(), a single request that reads several keys emits multiple misleading 'config loaded' lines implying one-time initialization when loading is actually per-access. (Underlying repeated disk+env read on every accessor is a performance issue deferred to critic-performance.) Fix: load once / cache, or downgrade this to debug.

_coerce treats only the literal strings 'true'/'false' as booleans, so common boolean spellings ('1','0','yes','no','on','off') are silently coerced differently ('1'->int 1, 'yes'->str 'yes'), giving inconsistent truthiness for what operators reasonably expect to be boolean flags. Fix: define an explicit boolean token set or document the accepted forms.
